snow_or_rain_notes: Snow present from base camp towards end of December through winter (December–February); upper reaches still under snow in March; snow melts away by April; no snow in summer (May–June) or autumn (September–November)
July and August are peak rainy months and trek is closed; June has occasional rainfall; monsoon sets in only in July
temperature_notes: Winter (Dec–Feb): Day 8–10 °C, Night 0 to -10 °C; 5 warm layers required
Spring (Mar–Apr): Day 15–18 °C, Night 0–5 °C; 3 warm layers required
Summer (May–Jun): Day 18–24 °C, Night 3–8 °C; 2 warm layers required
Autumn (Sep–Nov): Day 10–15 °C, Night 0 to -5 °C; 4 warm layers required

stated_difficulty: Challenging on the legs on summit day; requires a good level of fitness
experience_suitability: Trekkers with a good level of fitness
terrain_challenges: Hard, frozen overnight snow on summit day
Snowfall can block established paths requiring new tracks to be carved
Steep sections spread out across the trek
altitude_or_weather_risks: Rapid altitude gain of ~6,100 ft from Kotgaon (6,400 ft) to Kedarkantha summit (12,500 ft) in 4 trekking days
Risk of Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) above 12,000 ft
High-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers carried by Indiahikes team
Rainfall or snowfall lasting hours can significantly alter trek dynamics
Kotgaon stays in shade long after sunrise due to a blocking hill, making it one of the coldest base villages

fitness_benchmark: Walk, jog or run 5 km in under 40 minutes (minimum recommended fitness level)
approval_requirements: Screenshots must demonstrate meeting fitness criteria at least 3 times in the past month

experience_themes: Winter (December–February): snow-covered landscape, dense snow-covered forests, camping on thick mounds of snow, adventurous summit climb on thick snow blanket
Spring (March–April): last of the winter snow at upper reaches, Rhododendrons blooming, freshly exposed grasslands, riot of colours against snow backdrop, landscape coming alive after winter
Summer (May–June): bright mornings, lush green trek, less crowded, more isolation
Autumn (September–November): fresh meadows and forests after monsoon, crisp air, strikingly clear views of upper Himalayas, feeling of incoming winter by November
Winter: entire landscape and valley in white, snow-covered forests
Spring: Rhododendrons and freshly exposed grasslands blooming, flush of colours against snow backdrop, landscape coming alive after winter hibernation, riot of colours
primary_watchouts: Trek is closed in July and August due to peak monsoon/rainy months
Kedarkantha trek gets extremely crowded from the Sankri side starting December 15th until mid-January
Trek gets cold as it proceeds towards November with feeling of incoming winter
Trekkers visiting in July or August (trek closed due to peak monsoon)
Trekkers averse to extreme crowds from December 15 to mid-January on the Sankri side
best_for: Winter trekkers seeking snow-covered landscapes and adventurous summit climb
Beginners looking for a perfect winter trek combining beauty and adventure
Experienced trekkers wanting a memorable winter experience
Trekkers wanting isolation and fewer crowds (May–June)
Trekkers wanting clear Himalayan views and fresh post-monsoon landscapes (September–October)
not_ideal_for: Trekkers visiting in July or August (trek closed due to peak monsoon)
Trekkers averse to extreme crowds from December 15 to mid-January on the Sankri side
